speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of our interview with Mr. Leo Pop on November 21, 1995. So, Mr. Pop, you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was in 1959 that we got married. Like I say, my wife came over. We had to get married within 30 days. And we got married in Winnipeg. Of course, the wedding was done in the church because, you know, she was also from quite a religious family. And, well, it was just a small gathering. It was basically some people we knew from Winnipeg who attended. Her parents didn't go, so she was all by herself. And it was not that easy, but nevertheless, we made it and, you know, we got married. And then we moved into-- we rented in the-- the second floor in a townhouse where we lived. We had some furniture. She had brought quite a bit of furniture, or items for the kitchen and around the house, from Holland as presents, wedding presents, that arrived in a big box. And so we lived there from August '59 till, I guess it was in, ohh probably for six months. And then we kind of bought a house in Winnipeg where our oldest son was born in 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry. He's the oldest son, and he was born there in 1960. And then in 1962, we had another son, Sheldon. And in '63 we had one more son. It was Ian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had-- we had three boys. They-- we all lived in Winnipeg until 1965 at which time I-- I had an offer from Ontario Hydro for a job. And I worked in Winnipeg I-- at refine-- oil refinery originally. And then in the mean time, I had to study to get my Canadian engineering papers. And in 1963, I guess it was, that I got my profession in engineering status in Canada, which I have now.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was working at Manitoba Hydro. It was in the paper that Ontario Hydro was looking for engineers. And I applied. I came down here for an interview and shortly after I got a job offer, and we moved down to Ontario. That was in 1965. Not till now, we never regretted making the move to Toronto. First of all, it kind of got us away from the narrowness of the people in Winnipeg, what had basically all the narrowness in the religion and in the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start questioning yourself at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Because basically I consider myself Jewish. My parents died because they were Jewish, not because they were Christian, or whatever. They were Jewish. And I was, more or less, due to circumstances-- it's my own fault, probably, in the end-- but I-- I was brought up Christian. So I found a kind of, more or less, duty to follow whatever they had told me. And until we had our own family, our own-- my boys were still baptized. But then I started to question the religion and all this. And we finally broke with it, too, when we came here in '65. In 19-- I have to think-- 69, we had a daughter born here in Oakville. We lived here. So-- and so that-- and that made our family complete, three boys and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Yolanda Rachel. She has been quite involved in her Jewish heritage. She has gone and worked in Israel kibbutz. She went there for ulpan, originally, to study the language. She came back, and she went back again to Israel and worked in a kibbutz [NON-ENGLISH] at the-- for years again. And then she came back again. And it's still a weak spot for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite a few times. Yeah. We went, my wife and I went, already, I guess it was in the early '80s that we went for the first time. And I guess we I have been-- we have been together for a couple times. When my daughter was there I went to visit her, so rented a car, saw the whole country, and that was-- I have been also once. We met my aunt from Amsterdam. We flew from Amsterdam to Israel, so we met her there. We took also a tour of Israel. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel being in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a little strange but still I feel at home. It-- you know, it-- you have to get used to the way of living life there. But-- well, it's all basically Jewish heritage. We went, of course, to the Yad Vashem, what was not the nicest place to go to. But if you-- at least, I felt closer to my relatives, and my parents there than anywhere else. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So. Yeah. And, you know, this, especially having your own children, like I have mentioned before, see them grow up, you kind of realize what, you know, they-- they miss, too. Because they did not have any grandparents. You know, they had grandparents from my wife's side, but of course, they passed away later on. But my own parents they didn't have. And they consider more or less my aunt, what is-- who lives in Amsterdam, as their grandmother. So, you know, it's-- that's all that they really have for, you know, from my side. Excuse me. So. You know, I really feel that is-- you know, they haven't asked much about grandparents and all this, but they have missed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they ever question your heritage or background?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but they all know that-- you know, what it is. They consider themselves, you know, more or less Jewish, too. My daughter, especially, but my-- even my boys. You know. No, they haven't. They-- they know what has gone on. And-- but they've-- you know, and I felt that for them, too, that it is a real miss. You know, they missed something in their life because they never knew their grandparents. And, of course, seeing them grow up, like I mentioned earlier, I can see what I missed by being just by a single lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story today, Mr. Pop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I feel that it is-- well, it's at least the memory of my parents and of my other relatives who all perished through the-- in the-- during the Holocaust. When this came up, the Holocaust, although I was maybe not directly involved in the-- that way that I was in concentration camp, I feel my parents certainly deserve to be mentioned as such. They gave their life because they were Jewish. So did my grandparents, so did my uncle, my aunts, my nieces. And, you know, the terrible fact of what the Germans have done, you know, that should be mentioned, even in this case. And that's why I felt that I should also make, you know, make witness of this fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any specific or special message you'd like to leave to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean to-- to who?

speaker: interviewer
text: To your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my children. Well, first of all, I hope it never, you know, will happen again. You know, with the-- and, you know, I'm quite proud of my sons and my daughter, very proud, actually, also of my wife, who really has accepted me, although she is very-- from a very religious background herself. She has accepted me as being Jewish and, you know, we-- we had a nice-- we have-- we still have a very, you know, homogenized family as such. And I really am proud of that, what they all have achieved here. When I think back on, for example, if I look at my brother who lives still in Amsterdam. That's another thing I haven't mentioned, but because of the war, being grown up in separate ways, after the war, him being in orphanages and so on, he has had a very rough life, too. And we have grown apart completely. So that's-- but, you know, that's basically all I had. But it's still, you know, because of the war, we have grown apart. And if I go to Amsterdam sometimes, I see him, but you know, it's just not-- you know, it's like you meet anybody else. It's, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: He married and he got divorced. He has two sons, my brother. And one of them is in Israel, and he is, as far as I know, very religious. He is studying for rabbi, or whatever. And the other son lives in Amsterdam. I've met-- I have met them both, but he got separated. He divorced. And, so. But, you know, our lives go on their own ways. But we-- well, there's also from the-- from the war, of course. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, no. I-- Like I say, I just hope that this never will happen again. I'm still at the point that what I never can get over that this-- to forgive the Germans, no matter what it is. I'm still have full of hate for every German. And that will stay with me, I guess, till I die. I mean, sure, you have some good people among them. But in general, no. German is-- you know, they are the ones who did this, who brought it on to, you know, my parents, to the Jewish ways as such. That's what I-- why I have just no-- no good words for them at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Pop I want to thank you very much for giving testimony today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Here we see a picture what is from the Amsterdam archives. As you can see, this is my grandfather Jonas Vuisje. He was one of the ones who made the matzahs in-- around 1920. You can see the boxes right on the-- on the-- on the cart, what he is pulling. They were made for the Passover. It was all handmade, and they were distributed all over the city. He was one of the ones who kind of handed them-- brought them around the city. You can see the boxes in the-- on the cart. This must have been 1920, around there. You can see the old lights and the building was where they were made-- the matzahs were made and baked. It was in a Jewish district, what is all gone-- disappeared by now. So. This is the wedding picture of my father and mother, Solomon Pop and Anna Pop, that was done in 1933 in Amsterdam, where they got married. I don't know what the exact wedding date, but it was in Amsterdam in 1933. This is a picture where I attended kindergarten, probably taken around '38, '39. This was, as I mentioned before, the kindergarten was a private kindergarten that was located really just behind the building where eventually the Jewish people were all moved to and then put on transport. It's a coincidence how it just happened to be so close to the final place, more or less. This is a picture of my grandparents' home. My grandfather Leopold, the one I am called-- called after. This is my grandmother Judas, my parents, and an aunt what is Tante Bluma. She also perished in the Holocaust. This is done in 1935 at my grandparents' home in Amsterdam. Well, this picture, you can see my grandfather Leopold on the right with the smoke. That's the one I'm called after. Next to him is my father. Sitting down is my grandmother, Jetta. And to the-- her-- on the left is-- standing up is my mother. While the lady sitting in front is married to a brother of my father, and her name is Bluma. She is also one who had perished during the War. Was picked up with two little girls with the uncle who finally came back from the-- Poland from the coal mines. She never came back with the girls. Here you see a picture of what I have mentioned before, the wedding of my youngest brother of my mother in 1942. You can see we all had to wear the Star of David. To the right, you have my grandparents, what were the parents of the bridegroom and of my mother, Mr. and Mrs. Vuisje. In front of my grandfather is my niece, Rita. There in front of my grandmother is a little niece of the bride. Then you have the bride and bridegroom what is Harold and Annie Vuisje. Then you see her parents on the left. And in front of the mother of the bride you see myself with the Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was made in front of the synagogue, the Portuguese synagogue in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture what was made, I think it was around 1942 also, what shows myself-- my mother in the middle, my brother, and myself, what is made in the living room of our house in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: What side is that left?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother is on the left side, I am on the right side. I probably was around six, seven years old at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm sorry, where did you say this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the living room of our house in Amsterdam. Here we have two pictures, one is, again, this Tante Bluma I mentioned before. She is wearing the Star of David just before she was picked up and put on transport. You can see the two little girls with her. On the right is her husband, my uncle Arthur. He is the one who came back from the concentration camp in Poland where he was working in the coal mines. Tante Bluma and the girls never came back. Here we see another picture of the uncle who before got married in the-- in front of the synagogue, Uncle Harry with his two sons. The oldest son was born while he was with his wife in Theresienstadt concentration camp. When he came back in 1945, he had with him this-- the oldest son who was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which side of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is on the left side is Andre who was born, on the right side is my other-- his other son, Johnny. This is a picture that was taken in 1945 in a studio in Heerlen. When my aunt from Amsterdam came back, she wanted to have this picture of her remaining relatives. My left you see my younger brother Johnny. In the middle is my niece, Rita. And I'm on the right side. This is our wedding picture of Lammy and myself made on August the 12th, 1958. We got-- when we got married in the city of Winnipeg in Manitoba. This is a picture of my aunt, the only surviving sister of my mother, Aunt Dorothea in Amsterdam, who still call-- my boys all consider her as their grandmother as it is the only relative still remaining.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Dorothea's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last name is Vuisje, Dorothea Vuisje. V-U-I-S-J-E. She still lives today in Amsterdam and she is at the age of 84. This is a picture of my granddaughter, the oldest granddaughter, who is the old-- the daughter of my oldest son, Cortney She was born in November the 9th 19-- she just turned six, so that was 1989. We just saw Cortney. That is her sister, Katlin. These both the daughters of my older son, Henry. She was born on February the 9th, 1995. Here you see the cards, what we got after the war in 1944 issued by the city of Heerlen what gives you coupons, what gives you for clothing, and for food, and for-- for everything that you could get, what still was all stamp-- the stamps, like we can in [INAUDIBLE] in Canada and in the States-- food stamps, and so on. And this was an official document telling that I was registered now and where I was living. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Pop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a copy of a document that was issued by the city of Heerlen after I registered in 1944 when we were liberated. It tells you the name. It tells where I was born, where I came from, the religion, what, of course, was even NI, what is Dutch Israeli, or Netherlands Israeli. It was put in pencil out here for me, that's the kind of Reform church I went to. The other is where I was living and it's signed by someone from the city-- city hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Pop?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certificate, basically a death certificate, from the city of Amsterdam stating that my grandfather Leopold who was born in Wisch died on April the 2nd, 1943 in Sobibor. This is, more or less, a death certificate that I received from the city of Amsterdam. This is a copy of the birth certificate of my grandfather who was born in Wisch in 1981. And this, more or less, state that he was born there from the parents. And in the bottom is the signature of my great grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose name? I

speaker: interviewee
text: Wouldn't know. Johnny Pop? I just see the initials. I don't know his name because he must have died before-- but this is the signature. And that was done in 1980-- of 1881 in the city of Wisch. At that time, as you can see, they needed two witnesses to witness that he was born. So there's the two signatures in the bottom. This is a copy of the letter that I had mentioned before that was so-called written by my father to me after I just had left, more or less, the house in 1943. I have asked my aunt and even the style doesn't look like my father wrote it. So I suspect that this was written by someone from the underground in order to answer my letter what was given to me by what I sent to my parents. It basically reads, 'Dear Leo, we were very happy with your nice letter that I-- that we received. We are glad that you're having a nice holiday. It's so nice to hear that you saw your brother Johnny. Dear boy, the strong-- be strong and do your best to give your father and mother-- you will make them very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will see each other soon again. Is it nice where you are? I guess so. I will come to-- to see you soon. The weather is turning nice. Enjoy it'-- it was in June 1943. 'Mommy sends her love and also Tante Fannie, Uncle Mac, and Dickie'-- that is the neighbors on the second floor. 'Also, Oma Doortje and Opa Piet'-- what were the parents of my grandparents. They were still around then.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'I received your paint, and I will send this up to you. Dear Leo, keep well. Make sure your dad and mom will be-- can be proud of you, and we hope to hear good news from you. Write soon. A lot of kisses, and we will see you soon. Your dad and mom.' I am very happy and proud to introduce my family here. This is my wife, Lammy. This is my daughter, Yolanda Rachel. And here is my oldest son, Henry. Behind me is my son, Ian, the youngest son. And on the right is Sheldon, my middle son. My-- I'm quite proud that I have my four kids all here today with my wife and myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I can speak on behalf of all of us that we're very proud of my father to have done this. I guess throughout all of our childhood it's been very difficult to understand everything that's happened in his past and I myself, having been in Israel, have seen a lot. And I'm just very proud of him.
